title: Two faces of the bakery
summary: A Vienna night-baker works alone from one to six in the morning. The morning customers do not see the night customers — but Mr Eduard does.
body:
Mr Eduard bakes bread in a small Vienna bakery. He starts work at one in the morning. He finishes at six. He has done this since 1991. He works alone. The street is empty when he begins.

People sometimes ask him, 'Why nights? It is hard for the body.' He has a quiet answer. 'I do not bake the bread for the morning. I bake the bread for the night.'

This is the part most customers do not know. At three in the morning, the back door of the bakery is open. A woman from the hospital comes. She takes one warm loaf. She brings it to the night nurses. No one pays. They have an agreement that started thirty-two years ago, with the old baker.

At four, a man comes from the homeless shelter. Two loaves. No payment. The shelter cooks soup. Bread and soup.

At five, two taxi drivers come. They pay. They smile because their hands are cold. They eat in the taxi.

The day shop opens at six. The morning customers do not see the night customers.

Mr Eduard says, 'A bakery has two faces. The day face is the bread you buy. The night face is the bread you do not see.'

He goes home. He sleeps until evening.

annotations.meaningVi: mái ấm / nơi tạm trú (cho người vô gia cư)
làm việc một mình (không có đồng nghiệp)
cửa sau (của toà nhà hoặc tiệm)
Không ai trả tiền — diễn đạt thực tế không thanh toán
vô gia cư (không có nơi ở cố định)
vất vả / khó khăn cho cơ thể — collocation chỉ sự mệt nhọc thể chất
câu trả lời lặng lẽ / nhẹ nhàng — tính từ 'quiet' dùng cho giọng nói/thái độ
hai khuôn mặt / hai phía — dùng nghĩa bóng chỉ hai khía cạnh khác nhau
ngủ đến tận chiều — 'until' dùng để chỉ thời điểm kết thúc của hành động
y tá ca đêm — collocation chỉ nhân viên y tế làm việc ban đêm
ổ bánh còn ấm (colocation: warm + loaf — bánh vừa mới ra lò)

grammarNotes.title: Telling clock time with 'at'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'at + giờ' để chỉ thời điểm cụ thể trong ngày. 'at one in the morning' / 'at six' / 'at three in the afternoon'. Khi muốn rõ buổi, thêm 'in the morning / afternoon / evening' hoặc 'at night'. Lưu ý: 'at midnight', 'at noon', 'at night' đều dùng 'at', nhưng 'in the morning / afternoon / evening' dùng 'in'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'at + clock time' for a specific moment. 'at one in the morning', 'at six', 'at three in the afternoon'. Pair the time with 'in the morning/afternoon/evening' or 'at night' to make it clearer. Note: 'at midnight', 'at noon', and 'at night' use 'at', but parts of the day use 'in': 'in the morning'.

translationVi:
Ông Eduard làm bánh mì trong một tiệm bánh nhỏ ở Vienna. Ông bắt đầu làm việc lúc một giờ sáng. Ông xong lúc sáu giờ. Ông làm công việc này từ năm 1991. Ông làm một mình. Khi ông bắt đầu, đường phố vắng tanh.

Người ta đôi khi hỏi ông: 'Sao lại làm đêm? Vất cho cơ thể lắm.' Ông có một câu trả lời lặng lẽ. 'Tôi không làm bánh cho buổi sáng. Tôi làm bánh cho ban đêm.'

Đây là phần phần lớn khách hàng không biết. Lúc ba giờ sáng, cửa sau tiệm bánh mở. Một cô từ bệnh viện đến. Cô lấy một ổ bánh còn ấm. Cô mang về cho các y tá ca đêm. Không ai trả tiền. Họ có một thoả thuận bắt đầu từ ba mươi hai năm trước, với ông chủ tiệm cũ.

Lúc bốn giờ, một người đàn ông từ mái ấm cho người vô gia cư đến. Hai ổ. Không trả tiền. Mái ấm nấu súp. Bánh mì và súp.

Lúc năm giờ, hai bác tài xế taxi đến. Họ trả tiền. Họ cười vì tay đang lạnh. Họ ăn ngay trong xe.

Cửa hàng ban ngày mở lúc sáu giờ. Khách buổi sáng không nhìn thấy khách ban đêm.

Ông Eduard nói: 'Một tiệm bánh có hai khuôn mặt. Khuôn mặt ban ngày là ổ bánh bạn mua. Khuôn mặt ban đêm là ổ bánh bạn không thấy.'

Ông về nhà. Ông ngủ đến chiều.

questions.prompt: What hours does Mr Eduard work?
options: From six in the morning to one in the afternoon
From one in the morning to six in the morning
questions.explanation: 'He starts work at one in the morning. He finishes at six.'

questions.prompt: Since when has he done this work?
Who comes to the back door at three in the morning?
options: A woman from the hospital, who takes one warm loaf to the night nurses
questions.explanation: 'A woman from the hospital comes. She takes one warm loaf. She brings it to the night nurses.'

questions.prompt: How long has the hospital agreement been in place?
questions.explanation: 'They have an agreement that started thirty-two years ago, with the old baker.'
questions.hint: Look for the line about the old baker.

options: A man from the homeless shelter — for two loaves, with no payment
questions.explanation: 'At four, a man comes from the homeless shelter. Two loaves. No payment.'

questions.prompt: Who at the bakery actually pays for their bread?
options: Only the taxi drivers (at five), and the morning customers
questions.explanation: The hospital and the shelter receive bread free; 'two taxi drivers come. They pay.'
questions.hint: Compare the three groups before six o'clock.

questions.prompt: Why do the taxi drivers smile?
options: Their hands are cold, and the warm bread is welcome.
They have just won a prize.
The bread is free for them too.
questions.explanation: 'They smile because their hands are cold.'
questions.hint: Look at the 'because' clause about the taxi drivers.

questions.prompt: What does the day shop open at?
questions.hint: Look for the shop opening time.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Eduard mean by the 'two faces' of the bakery?
options: The day face is the bread you buy; the night face is the bread you do not see.
questions.explanation: He quotes the two faces directly.
questions.hint: Look at his quoted lines near the end.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Some of the most important work a small business does is invisible to its paying customers.
Night work is healthier than day work.
Bakeries should always be open all night.
Vienna has the best bread in Europe.
questions.explanation: The morning customers never see the hospital or shelter — but Mr Eduard does.
questions.hint: Think about what 'face' the regular customers see.

cloze.template:
Mr Eduard bakes bread in a small Vienna bakery. He starts work at {{b1}} in the morning. He finishes at six. He has done this since 1991. He works alone. The street is {{b2}} when he begins.

People sometimes ask him, 'Why nights? It is hard for the body.' He has a quiet answer. 'I do not bake the bread for the morning. I bake the bread for the {{b3}}.'

This is the part most customers do not know. At {{b4}} in the morning, the back door of the bakery is open. A woman from the hospital comes. She takes one warm {{b5}}. She brings it to the night nurses. No one pays. They have an agreement that started thirty-{{b6}} years ago, with the old baker.

At four, a man comes from the homeless {{b7}}. Two loaves. No payment. The shelter cooks soup. Bread and soup.

At five, two taxi drivers come. They pay. They smile because their {{b8}} are cold. They eat in the taxi.

The day shop opens at six. The morning customers do not see the {{b9}} customers.

Mr Eduard says, 'A bakery has two faces. The day face is the bread you buy. The {{b10}} face is the bread you do not see.'

He goes home. He sleeps until evening.

blanks.explanation: He starts at one in the morning.
The street is empty so early.
The morning customers do not see the night customers.
The night face is the invisible one.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Eduard's most meaningful customers are invisible to the people who pay him. Is there a part of your work, school, or family life where the most important effort never gets seen by the people you serve? What is gained — and what is lost — by that invisibility?
